title: Benchmarking the ORCA PT-2 Boson Sampler using Minimum Dominating Set Problems
authors: Jessica Park, Susan Stepney, Irene D'Amico
abstract: We use boson sampling as part of a gradient-free variational algorithm (the Binary Bosonic Solver) to solve a minimum dominating set problem and compare these results to a number of exact and heuristic classical algorithms. The boson sampling has been performed on the physical PT-2 time-bin interferometer from ORCA Computing. The PT-2 device has been tested here using both a single- and double-loop configuration and the results are compared based on the best found solution and the overall run time. With the parameters used in this experiment, the boson sampler is outperformed by the classical methods, but we hypothesise that this is due to insufficient samples and iterations. We classically simulate boson sampling in a single-loop configuration to break down the runtime for individual algorithmic components, allowing for estimates of when boson sampling may outperform classical methods. This study recommends a watching brief on boson sampling as the complexity of the interferometer is improved and the loss in the hardware is reduced allowing for better performance from the associated algorithms.
summary: This work benchmarks using boson sampling on a physical photonic interferometer (PT-2) to solve the Minimum Dominating Set problem. The authors compare this quantum approach against several classical algorithms, finding that current performance is limited by insufficient sampling resources. The study is important for guiding future hardware development by identifying the necessary scaling of resources for quantum advantage.
main_problem: The paper benchmarks the use of boson sampling, implemented on a physical quantum device, to solve the NP-hard Minimum Dominating Set problem.
main_result: While the boson sampler is currently outperformed by classical algorithms with limited resources, the study validates the simulation process and suggests that performance will improve as hardware complexity and loss are reduced.
method: The authors compare the results of the Binary Bosonic Solver (BBS) on the physical PT-2 device against established classical heuristics and exact solvers.
model_or_system: The experiment uses the physical PT-2 time-bin interferometer from ORCA Computing, which samples from non-Gaussian squeezed states. The problem solved is the Minimum Dominating Set problem on a graph $G(n, p, s)$.
key_observables: Best found solution quality (size of the dominating set) and overall wall-clock runtime.
important_parameters: The number of samples ($N_{Samp}$) and iterations ($N_{Iter}$) are critical parameters whose insufficient values are hypothesized to limit current performance.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that the current performance gap is due to insufficient sampling and iteration counts, not a fundamental limitation of the quantum approach.
figures_summary: Figures compare runtimes across various methods, show the scaling of unique cost function evaluations, and compare simulated vs. real hardware sampling times for the interferometer.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem and platform, details the comparison between the quantum (BBS) and classical solvers, presents results comparing solution quality and runtime, and concludes with recommendations for future resource scaling.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, near-term benchmark for quantum advantage by applying boson sampling to a known hard problem, directly relevant to photonic quantum computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Diagrammatic representation of time-bin interferometry. Taken from Bradler and Wallner (2021).

Fig. 2: A visual representation of the Binary Bosonic Solver run in practice on the PT- 2 device with the various timing metrics labelled. titer is the time for a single iterations; tserv is the time taken accessing the PT-2 device including classical overheads and sampling; tS is the time for an individual sample to be generated from the PT-2 device; tC is the time taken for the local CPU to evaluate the cost function for a single bit string instance.

Fig. 3: Comparisons between the found solution size of three classical methods for solving the dominating set problem (Greedy, NX Approximation and PULP) and three implementations of the binary bosonic solver (BBS); a 1 loop CPU simulation (BBS 1-loop Sim), a 1-loop PT-2 implementation (BBS 1-loop PT-2), and a 2-loop PT-2 implementation (BBS 2-loop PT-2). The lines represent the mean value over three different graphs (for each value of n), and the shaded areas shows the minimum and maximum of results.
Fig. 4: The cumulative number of unique cost function evaluations carried out as the Binary Bosonic Solver progresses through the iterations, for different problem sizes.

Figure 5 shows the wall-clock runtime for the three classical algorithms, and the three BBS implementations (1-loop Sim, 1-loop PT-2 and 2-loop PT-2). There are also lines that show the estimated convergence time as calculated by 4 for both the real and simulated PT-2 device running the gradient-free BBS algorithm. Overall the runtime for the BBS algorithm is higher than that of the classical methods whether being enacted on a CPU simulation or with the real PT-2 device. Although higher than the classical methods, the runtime of the BBS simulations in this experiment are approximately an order of magnitude smaller than shown in the previous study (Park et al., 2026).
